guides.title: How to Install and Use Nmap
guides.description: This guide covers installing Nmap, running your first scan, understanding output, and using common scan types for network reconnaissance.
prerequisites: A target you have permission to scan

steps.title: Install Nmap
steps.description: Install Nmap using your system's package manager.

steps.title: Run Your First Scan
steps.description: Start with a simple scan against a single target. This performs a SYN scan on the 1000 most common ports.
steps.output:
Starting Nmap 7.94 ( https://nmap.org )
Nmap scan report for scanme.nmap.org (45.33.32.156)
Host is up (0.089s latency).
Not shown: 996 closed tcp ports
PORT      STATE SERVICE
22/tcp    open  ssh
80/tcp    open  http
9929/tcp  open  nping-echo
31337/tcp open  Elite
steps.notes: scanme.nmap.org is a server provided by the Nmap project specifically for testing. Don't scan targets you don't have permission to test.

steps.title: Service and Version Detection
steps.description: Use the -sV flag to detect what software and version is running on each open port. This is essential for identifying potentially vulnerable services.
steps.notes: OS detection (-O) requires root/sudo privileges because it uses raw packets.

steps.title: Scan Specific Ports
steps.description: Target specific ports or port ranges instead of the default top 1000.
steps.notes: Scanning all 65535 ports (-p-) takes significantly longer but catches services running on non-standard ports.

steps.title: NSE Scripts
steps.description: Nmap's Scripting Engine (NSE) has hundreds of scripts for vulnerability detection, service enumeration, and more.
steps.notes: The -sC flag runs default scripts which are safe and informative. The vuln category scripts actively check for vulnerabilities - only use these on targets you're authorized to test.

steps.title: Save Output
steps.description: Save scan results in different formats for later analysis and reporting.
steps.notes: XML output (-oX) can be imported into tools like Metasploit, EyeWitness, and many reporting platforms.

guides.title: How to Install and Use Sherlock
guides.description: This guide covers installing Sherlock and using it to find usernames across hundreds of social media platforms and websites.

steps.title: Install Sherlock
steps.description: Install Sherlock from pip or clone the repository.

steps.title: Search for a Username
steps.description: Run a basic search for a username across all supported sites.
steps.output:
[*] Checking username targetusername on:
[+] GitHub: https://github.com/targetusername
[+] Twitter: https://twitter.com/targetusername
[+] Instagram: https://instagram.com/targetusername
steps.notes: Replace 'targetusername' with the username you're investigating. Sherlock checks hundreds of sites, so it takes a minute or two.

steps.title: Search Multiple Usernames
steps.description: Check several usernames in one run - useful when a target uses different handles on different platforms.

steps.title: Save Results
steps.description: Export results to a file for documentation and reporting.

steps.title: Filter and Refine
steps.description: Use flags to narrow your search or handle rate limiting.
steps.notes: Some sites rate-limit or block automated requests. If you get false negatives, try increasing the timeout or using the --tor flag if you have Tor installed.

guides.title: How to Install and Use Nuclei
guides.description: This guide covers installing Nuclei, running your first vulnerability scan, using templates, and building automated scanning workflows.
prerequisites: Go 1.21+ (for go install) or download binary directly
A target URL you have permission to scan

steps.title: Install Nuclei
steps.description: Install Nuclei and download the template library.
steps.notes: On first run, Nuclei automatically downloads thousands of community templates from the nuclei-templates repository.

steps.title: Run Your First Scan
steps.description: Scan a single URL with all default templates. This checks for thousands of known vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, and exposed files.
steps.notes: A full template scan can take several minutes. Nuclei is noisy - only scan targets you have explicit authorization to test.

steps.title: Filter by Severity
steps.description: Focus on what matters by filtering templates by severity level.

steps.title: Scan Multiple Targets
steps.description: Pipe a list of URLs into Nuclei for bulk scanning. This is how most real assessments work - you feed in URLs from tools like httpx or subfinder.
steps.notes: Rate limiting (-rate-limit) is important to avoid overwhelming targets or getting blocked. 50 requests per second is a reasonable starting point.

steps.title: Use Specific Template Categories
steps.description: Run only specific types of checks instead of everything.

steps.title: Save and Format Output
steps.description: Export findings in different formats for reporting.
steps.notes: The -me flag generates a markdown report directory with findings organized by severity. JSON output is best for feeding into other tools or dashboards.

guides.title: How to Install and Use Metasploit
guides.description: This guide covers installing the Metasploit Framework, navigating the console, searching for exploits, and running your first exploit against a test target.
prerequisites: A deliberately vulnerable target like Metasploitable for practice

steps.title: Install Metasploit
steps.description: On Kali Linux, Metasploit is pre-installed. For other systems, use the installer script.

steps.title: Launch the Console
steps.description: Start the Metasploit console. First launch initializes the database which takes a moment.
steps.notes: The database stores your scan results, credentials, and session history across sessions. Always initialize it before starting.

steps.title: Search for Modules
steps.description: Metasploit has thousands of exploit, auxiliary, and post-exploitation modules. Search by keyword, CVE, or platform.

steps.title: Use a Module
steps.description: Select a module, configure its options, and understand what it does before running it.
steps.notes: Always review options with 'show options' before running. RHOSTS is the target, LHOST is your machine. Never run exploits against systems you don't have explicit written authorization to test.

steps.title: Run and Interact
steps.description: Execute the module and interact with any sessions you get.

steps.title: Auxiliary Modules
steps.description: Not everything is an exploit. Auxiliary modules handle scanning, enumeration, and information gathering.

commands.label: Port scanner
commands.command:
use auxiliary/scanner/portscan/tcp
set RHOSTS 192.168.1.0/24
set PORTS 22,80,443,445
run

steps.notes: Auxiliary modules are great for the reconnaissance phase. They're less invasive than exploits and help you understand the target environment.

guides.title: How to Install and Use Hashcat
guides.description: This guide covers installing Hashcat, identifying hash types, running dictionary attacks, and using rules for effective password cracking.
prerequisites: A GPU with up-to-date drivers (NVIDIA CUDA or AMD ROCm)
Hashcat supports CPU-only mode but GPU is 10-100x faster

steps.title: Install Hashcat
steps.description: Install Hashcat from your package manager or download the latest release.
steps.notes: hashcat -I shows detected compute devices. If your GPU doesn't appear, check your driver installation. On macOS, Metal is supported natively.

steps.title: Identify the Hash Type
steps.description: Before cracking, you need to know what type of hash you're working with. Hashcat uses numeric mode codes for each hash type.
steps.notes: Use the tool 'name-that-hash' or 'hashid' if you don't know what type of hash you have. NTLM hashes (from Windows) are mode 1000.

steps.title: Dictionary Attack
steps.description: The most common attack mode. Try every word in a wordlist against the hash.
steps.notes: rockyou.txt is the classic starter wordlist with 14 million passwords. On Kali it's at /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt.gz - unzip it first.

steps.title: Rule-Based Attack
steps.description: Rules mutate each word in the wordlist - adding numbers, capitalizing letters, replacing characters. This massively increases coverage without needing a bigger wordlist.
steps.notes: best64.rule is a great starting point - it's fast and catches common patterns. dive.rule is more aggressive. The community 'OneRule' combines the best from multiple rule sets.

steps.title: Mask Attack (Brute Force with Patterns)
steps.description: When you know the password structure (like 'starts with uppercase, ends with 4 digits'), mask attacks are more efficient than pure brute force.
steps.notes: Mask charsets: ?l=lowercase ?u=uppercase ?d=digit ?s=special ?a=all. You can define custom charsets with -1, -2, -3, -4.

steps.title: Session Management
steps.description: Long cracking jobs can be paused and resumed. Always use session names for important jobs.

commands.label: Check status during run
commands.command: # Press 's' during a running session for status

guides.title: How to Install and Use sqlmap
guides.description: This guide covers installing sqlmap, testing for SQL injection, extracting databases, and using it effectively during web application assessments.
prerequisites: A target URL with parameters you have permission to test

steps.title: Install sqlmap
steps.description: Install sqlmap from your package manager or clone from GitHub.

steps.title: Test a URL for SQL Injection
steps.description: Point sqlmap at a URL with a parameter and let it test for injection.
steps.notes: sqlmap will ask you questions during the scan - usually the defaults are fine. Add --batch to auto-accept defaults for unattended scans.

steps.title: Enumerate Databases
steps.description: Once sqlmap confirms injection, enumerate the database structure.

steps.title: Extract Data
steps.description: Dump specific tables or columns from the database.
steps.notes: sqlmap will automatically attempt to crack password hashes it finds using its built-in dictionary. You can also export hashes to crack with hashcat.

steps.title: POST Request and Form Testing
steps.description: Test POST parameters and form submissions, not just URL parameters.
steps.notes: The -r flag reads a full HTTP request saved from Burp Suite. This is the easiest way to test complex requests with cookies, headers, and custom content types.

steps.title: Useful Flags
steps.description: Common flags for real-world usage.
steps.notes: Level 1-5 controls how many parameters/injection points to test. Risk 1-3 controls how dangerous the payloads are. Level 3 risk 2 is a good balance for thorough testing.

guides.title: How to Install and Use Masscan
guides.description: This guide covers installing Masscan, running fast port scans across large IP ranges, and piping results into Nmap for detailed analysis.
prerequisites: Root/sudo access (required for raw packet sending)
A target range you have permission to scan

steps.title: Install Masscan
steps.description: Install from package manager or build from source for the latest version.

steps.title: Run a Basic Scan
steps.description: Scan common ports across a target range. Masscan requires root because it sends raw packets.
steps.output:
Discovered open port 22/tcp on 192.168.1.10
Discovered open port 80/tcp on 192.168.1.1
Discovered open port 443/tcp on 192.168.1.50
steps.notes: The --rate flag controls packets per second. 1000 is conservative. Masscan can do 10 million pps but that will overwhelm most networks - start low.

steps.title: Scan Large Ranges
steps.description: Masscan's strength is speed across large IP ranges. Scan entire subnets in seconds.
steps.notes: Always maintain an exclude file for IP ranges you must not scan (shared infrastructure, out-of-scope assets). One wrong scan can end an engagement.

steps.title: Save Output for Nmap
steps.description: The classic workflow: Masscan finds open ports fast, then Nmap does detailed service detection on the results.

commands.label: Parse and feed to Nmap
commands.command:
sudo masscan 192.168.1.0/24 -p 1-65535 --rate=1000 -oG results.gnmap
grep open results.gnmap | awk '{print $2}' | sort -u > live-hosts.txt
nmap -sV -iL live-hosts.txt

steps.title: Banners and Service Detection
steps.description: Masscan can grab basic banners from services it discovers.
steps.notes: Masscan's banner grabbing is basic compared to Nmap's service detection, but it's much faster for initial triage. Use it to identify what's there, then follow up with Nmap for detail.

guides.title: How to Install and Use Trivy
guides.description: This guide covers installing Trivy and scanning container images, filesystems, and git repos for vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, and secrets.

steps.title: Install Trivy
steps.description: Install Trivy from your package manager or download the binary.

steps.title: Scan a Container Image
steps.description: Scan a Docker image for known vulnerabilities in OS packages and application dependencies.
steps.notes: Trivy downloads vulnerability databases on first run. It scans both OS-level packages (apt, apk, rpm) and language-specific dependencies (pip, npm, gem, etc.).

steps.title: Scan Your Project Directory
steps.description: Scan a local filesystem for vulnerabilities in lock files, IaC misconfigurations, and exposed secrets.

steps.title: Scan a Git Repository
steps.description: Scan a remote git repository without cloning it first.

steps.title: CI/CD Integration
steps.description: Use Trivy in your build pipeline to catch vulnerabilities before deployment.
steps.notes: The --exit-code 1 flag makes Trivy return a non-zero exit code when it finds vulnerabilities at the specified severity, which fails your CI pipeline. This is how you gate deployments on security.

guides.title: How to Install and Use Gitleaks
guides.description: This guide covers installing Gitleaks and scanning git repositories for accidentally committed secrets, API keys, and credentials.
prerequisites: Go (for go install) or download binary

steps.title: Install Gitleaks
steps.description: Install Gitleaks from Homebrew, Go, or download the binary.

steps.title: Scan a Local Repository
steps.description: Scan the current git repository including all commit history for secrets.
steps.output:
Finding:     AKIA...
Secret:      AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE
RuleID:      aws-access-key-id
File:        config/deploy.sh
Commit:      abc1234...
steps.notes: Gitleaks scans the entire git history by default, not just current files. Secrets that were committed and then deleted are still found.

steps.title: Scan Staged Changes Only (Pre-Commit)
steps.description: Use as a pre-commit hook to catch secrets before they're committed.
steps.notes: The protect command only checks staged changes, making it fast enough for a pre-commit hook. This catches secrets before they enter the git history.

steps.title: Scan a Remote Repository
steps.description: Clone and scan a remote repository in one step.

steps.title: Custom Configuration
steps.description: Customize what Gitleaks looks for with a config file, or exclude known false positives.
steps.notes: The baseline feature is useful for legacy repos with known committed secrets that can't be rotated. Generate a baseline once, then future scans only report new findings.

guides.title: How to Install and Use TruffleHog
guides.description: This guide covers installing TruffleHog and scanning git repos, filesystems, S3 buckets, and more for verified secrets and credentials.
prerequisites: Go (for go install) or download binary

steps.title: Install TruffleHog
steps.description: Install TruffleHog from Homebrew, pip, or download the binary.

steps.title: Scan a Git Repository
steps.description: Scan a repo's entire commit history for secrets. TruffleHog's key differentiator is that it verifies found secrets against the actual service to confirm they're live.
steps.notes: The --only-verified flag is powerful - it actually tests found credentials against AWS, GitHub, Slack, etc. to confirm they work. This dramatically reduces false positives.

steps.title: Scan a Filesystem
steps.description: Scan a directory for secrets without git history.

steps.title: Scan GitHub Organizations
steps.description: Scan all repositories in a GitHub organization. Requires a GitHub token.
steps.notes: Scanning an entire org is one of the most valuable things you can do for an organization's security posture. Many breaches start with leaked credentials in repos.

steps.title: JSON Output and CI Integration
steps.description: Output results in JSON for automation and CI/CD pipelines.
steps.notes: Use --fail with --only-verified in CI to block deployments only when actually live secrets are found. This avoids blocking on historical or revoked credentials.

guides.title: How to Install and Use Bitcoin Core CLI
guides.description: This guide covers installing Bitcoin Core, syncing the blockchain, and using bitcoin-cli to query transactions, blocks, and addresses.
prerequisites: 500GB+ free disk space for full blockchain sync
Patience - initial sync takes hours to days

steps.title: Install Bitcoin Core
steps.description: Download and install Bitcoin Core which includes both the daemon (bitcoind) and the CLI tool (bitcoin-cli).

commands.label: Ubuntu
commands.command: sudo apt install snapd && sudo snap install bitcoin-core

steps.title: Start the Daemon
steps.description: Start bitcoind to begin syncing the blockchain. First sync takes a long time.
steps.notes: For investigations where you don't need the full blockchain locally, consider using -prune=10000 to keep only the most recent 10GB of blocks. Or use a public API for quick lookups.

steps.title: Query Blockchain Info
steps.description: Once synced, query blocks, transactions, and network info.

steps.title: Look Up Transactions
steps.description: Query specific transactions by their hash. This is the core of blockchain forensics - following the money.
steps.notes: The 'true' parameter on getrawtransaction returns decoded JSON instead of raw hex. You need -txindex=1 in your bitcoin.conf to look up arbitrary transactions (not just ones in your wallet).

steps.title: Wallet Operations (for Testing)
steps.description: Create a wallet and generate addresses for testing and research.
steps.notes: For forensic work you typically don't need a wallet - you're analyzing other people's transactions. But having a wallet is useful for understanding how the protocol works.

guides.title: How to Install and Use SecLists
guides.description: This guide covers downloading SecLists and using its wordlists with tools like ffuf, Gobuster, Hashcat, and Nuclei for effective security testing.
prerequisites: A security testing tool that accepts wordlists (ffuf, gobuster, hashcat, etc.)

steps.title: Install SecLists
steps.description: Download the SecLists collection. On Kali Linux it's pre-installed.
steps.notes: SecLists is about 1GB downloaded. It's a collection of wordlists, not a tool itself - you use these lists with other tools.

steps.title: Directory Brute-Forcing
steps.description: Use SecLists' Discovery wordlists with ffuf or Gobuster to find hidden directories and files on web servers.
steps.notes: Start with common.txt (4,700 entries) for quick scans. Move to directory-list-2.3-medium.txt (220,000 entries) for thorough coverage. The API wordlists are gold for testing REST APIs.

steps.title: Subdomain Enumeration
steps.description: Use DNS wordlists for subdomain brute-forcing.

steps.title: Password Attacks
steps.description: SecLists includes curated password lists and username lists for credential testing.
steps.notes: The Default-Credentials directory contains default username:password combos for hundreds of products - routers, databases, web apps. Check these first before brute-forcing.

steps.title: What's Inside
steps.description: A quick tour of the most useful directories in SecLists.
steps.notes: Key directories: Discovery/Web-Content/ (directory bruting), Discovery/DNS/ (subdomain lists), Passwords/ (credential lists), Fuzzing/ (injection payloads for XSS, SQLi, etc.), Usernames/ (username lists). The Fuzzing directory is especially useful for manual testing - it has payloads for every injection type.

guides.title: How to Install and Use Ghidra
guides.description: This guide covers installing Ghidra, loading a binary, navigating the decompiler, and performing basic reverse engineering analysis.

steps.title: Install Ghidra
steps.description: Download Ghidra from the official GitHub releases. It's a portable application - no installation required.
steps.notes: Ghidra is developed by the NSA and released as open source. Despite the source, it's genuinely excellent and widely trusted by the security community.

steps.title: Create a Project and Import a Binary
steps.description: Ghidra organizes work into projects. Create one, then import the binary you want to analyze.

commands.label: GUI steps
commands.command:
# File > New Project > Non-Shared Project
# Choose a directory and project name
# File > Import File > Select your binary
# Double-click the imported file to open in CodeBrowser

steps.notes: Ghidra supports PE (Windows), ELF (Linux), Mach-O (macOS), APK, firmware images, and many other formats. It auto-detects the format and architecture.

steps.title: Auto Analysis
steps.description: When you open a binary, Ghidra offers to run auto-analysis. Accept it - this identifies functions, strings, cross-references, and data types.

commands.label: GUI steps
commands.command:
# Click 'Yes' when prompted for auto-analysis
# Use default analysis options
# Wait for analysis to complete (watch the progress bar)

steps.notes: Auto-analysis can take a few minutes for large binaries. It's doing function identification, disassembly, decompilation prep, and string extraction. Let it finish before you start exploring.

steps.title: Navigate the Decompiler
steps.description: The decompiler window shows C-like pseudocode for the selected function. This is Ghidra's killer feature.

commands.label: Key navigation
commands.command:
# Click any function in the listing to see its decompiled code
# Double-click function calls to follow them
# Press 'G' to go to a specific address
# Use Window > Defined Strings to find all strings
# Right-click a function > Rename to give it a meaningful name

steps.notes: Start with the Defined Strings window - searching for strings like 'password', 'error', 'http://', or 'key' often leads you straight to interesting functions. Renaming functions as you understand them makes the code progressively more readable.

steps.title: Cross-References and Function Graphs
steps.description: Understand how functions relate to each other using cross-references and control flow graphs.

commands.label: Key features
commands.command:
# Right-click any function/variable > References > Show References To
# Window > Function Graph (visual control flow)
# Window > Function Call Graph (who calls what)
# Search > For Strings (find specific text in the binary)

steps.notes: Cross-references (xrefs) answer 'who calls this function?' and 'where is this string used?' - these are your primary navigation tools when analyzing unknown binaries.

steps.title: Scripting with Ghidra
steps.description: Ghidra has a built-in script manager for automating analysis tasks in Java or Python.

commands.label: Open script manager
commands.command:
# Window > Script Manager
# Browse existing scripts by category
# Click the green play button to run a script
# Or write your own in Java/Python (Jython)

steps.notes: The built-in scripts include useful utilities like 'FindCrypt' (identifies crypto constants), 'SearchForStrings', and various deobfuscation helpers. The scripting API is well-documented in Ghidra's built-in help.

guides.title: How to Install and Use CyberChef
guides.description: This guide covers accessing CyberChef, building transformation recipes, and using it for common security analysis tasks like decoding, decryption, and data extraction.
prerequisites: A web browser (that's it - CyberChef runs entirely in the browser)

steps.title: Access CyberChef
steps.description: CyberChef runs in your browser. Use the hosted version or run it locally for sensitive data.

commands.label: Run locally with Docker
commands.command:
docker run -d -p 8000:8000 ghcr.io/gchq/cyberchef:latest
# Then visit http://localhost:8000

steps.notes: CyberChef runs entirely client-side - no data is sent to any server, even on the hosted version. But for sensitive investigation data, running locally is best practice.

steps.title: Your First Recipe
steps.description: CyberChef works by chaining operations into a 'recipe'. Paste input on the left, drag operations into the recipe area, see output on the right.

commands.label: Decode Base64
commands.command:
# 1. Paste Base64 text in the Input box
# 2. Search for 'From Base64' in the Operations panel
# 3. Drag it into the Recipe area
# 4. Output appears automatically

# Try it: paste 'SGVsbG8gV29ybGQ=' and apply From Base64

steps.title: Chain Multiple Operations
steps.description: The real power is chaining operations. Data flows through each step in order.

commands.label: Base64 then hex decode
commands.command:
# Recipe:
# 1. From Base64
# 2. From Hex

# URL decode then Base64 decode:
# 1. URL Decode
# 2. From Base64

commands.label: Extract and decode
commands.command:
# Recipe for extracting URLs from encoded data:
# 1. From Base64
# 2. Extract URLs

steps.notes: Drag operations to reorder them. Each operation transforms the output of the previous one. You can disable individual steps by clicking the checkbox to debug your recipe.

steps.title: Common Security Recipes
steps.description: Recipes you'll use regularly in security work.

commands.label: Decode XOR-encoded malware string
commands.command:
# 1. From Hex
# 2. XOR (set the key)
# Output: decoded string

commands.label: Analyze JWT token
commands.command:
# 1. Paste the JWT
# 2. JWT Decode
# Shows header, payload, and signature

commands.label: Hash identification
commands.command:
# 1. Paste a hash
# 2. Analyse hash
# Suggests the hash type

commands.label: Extract IOCs from a report
commands.command:
# 1. Paste text
# 2. Extract IP addresses
# 3. Sort
# 4. Unique

steps.title: The Magic Button
steps.description: CyberChef's Magic operation automatically detects encoding and suggests transformations. It's surprisingly good.

commands.label: Auto-detect encoding
commands.command:
# 1. Paste encoded/encrypted data in Input
# 2. Search for 'Magic' in Operations
# 3. Drag it into Recipe
# 4. CyberChef suggests what encoding it is and how to decode it

steps.notes: Magic works by trying common transformations and checking if the output looks like readable text. It can chain multiple steps automatically - sometimes revealing Base64-within-URL-encoding-within-hex patterns you wouldn't spot manually.

guides.title: How to Install and Use Rclone
guides.description: This guide covers installing Rclone and using it to sync, copy, and mount cloud storage from 70+ providers including S3, Google Drive, and Azure Blob.

steps.title: Install Rclone
steps.description: Install Rclone from your package manager or the official script.

steps.title: Configure a Remote
steps.description: Set up a connection to a cloud storage provider. The interactive config walks you through it.
steps.notes: Follow the prompts to set up S3, Google Drive, Dropbox, SFTP, or any of 70+ supported providers. Each remote gets a name you'll reference in commands.

steps.title: Basic Operations
steps.description: Copy, sync, and list files on your configured remotes.
steps.notes: sync makes the destination match the source exactly - it will delete files at the destination that don't exist at the source. Use copy if you don't want deletions.

steps.title: Mount as Filesystem
steps.description: Mount a remote as a local filesystem. Access cloud storage like a local folder.
steps.notes: Mounting is great for browsing cloud storage with normal file tools. Performance depends on your connection speed.

steps.title: Security-Relevant Usage
steps.description: Rclone is commonly used for data exfiltration simulation in pentests and for forensic evidence collection from cloud storage.

commands.label: Encrypted remote
commands.command:
# During rclone config, choose 'crypt' as the remote type
# This adds client-side encryption to any other remote

steps.notes: In red team contexts, rclone's ability to sync to attacker-controlled cloud storage makes it a common exfiltration tool. Defenders should monitor for rclone.exe and its network signatures.

guides.title: How to Install and Use Geth
guides.description: This guide covers installing Geth (Go Ethereum), syncing the Ethereum blockchain, and querying accounts, transactions, and smart contracts.

steps.title: Install Geth
steps.description: Install Geth from package manager or download the binary.

commands.label: Ubuntu PPA
commands.command: sudo add-apt-repository -y ppa:ethereum/ethereum && sudo apt update && sudo apt install geth

steps.title: Start Syncing
steps.description: Start Geth in snap sync mode (fastest way to get a usable node).
steps.notes: Snap sync downloads block headers and state data in parallel. It's much faster than full sync. The --http flags enable the JSON-RPC API for querying.

steps.title: Query the Blockchain
steps.description: Use the Geth JavaScript console to query accounts, balances, and transactions.
steps.notes: Balances are returned in Wei (1 ETH = 10^18 Wei). Use web3.fromWei(balance, 'ether') to convert.

steps.title: JSON-RPC API
steps.description: Query Geth programmatically via the HTTP JSON-RPC API.
steps.notes: The JSON-RPC API is how most tools (Ethers.js, Web3.py, Cast) communicate with Geth. Running your own node means you're not dependent on Infura or Alchemy rate limits.

guides.title: How to Install and Use x64dbg
guides.description: This guide covers installing x64dbg on Windows and using it for basic binary debugging, breakpoints, and malware analysis.

steps.title: Install x64dbg
steps.description: Download and extract x64dbg. It's portable - no installer needed.

commands.label: Download
commands.command:
# Download latest snapshot from https://x64dbg.com
# Extract the zip to a folder like C:\x64dbg
# Run x96dbg.exe (launcher that picks 32 or 64-bit)

steps.notes: x96dbg.exe is the launcher that auto-selects x32dbg or x64dbg based on the target binary's architecture.

steps.title: Load a Binary
steps.description: Open a binary for debugging. x64dbg pauses at the system entry point.

commands.label: GUI
commands.command:
# File > Open > Select your executable
# The debugger pauses at ntdll entry point
# Press F9 (Run) to reach the application entry point

steps.title: Set Breakpoints
steps.description: Breakpoints pause execution so you can inspect the program's state at critical points.

commands.label: Key commands
commands.command:
# F2 - Toggle breakpoint at current line
# Ctrl+G - Go to address
# F7 - Step into (follow function calls)
# F8 - Step over (execute call, stop at next line)
# F9 - Run until next breakpoint

steps.notes: For malware analysis, set breakpoints on API calls like CreateFile, WriteFile, RegSetValue, connect, and send to understand what the malware does.

steps.title: Inspect Memory and Registers
steps.description: View register values, memory contents, and the stack while debugging.

commands.label: Key windows
commands.command:
# CPU tab - Disassembly, registers, stack, memory dump
# Memory Map tab - See all loaded modules and memory regions
# Handles tab - Open file handles, registry keys, network sockets
# Strings tab - Right-click module > Search for > All strings

steps.title: Useful Plugins
steps.description: x64dbg has a plugin ecosystem. A few essentials for security work.

commands.label: Essential plugins
commands.command:
# ScyllaHide - Anti-anti-debug (hides debugger from detection)
# x64dbgpy - Python scripting support
# Swiss Army Knife - Collection of useful utilities
# SharpOD - Another anti-debug plugin

# Install: drop DLLs into x64dbg/release/x32/plugins/ or x64/plugins/

steps.notes: Malware commonly checks for debuggers using IsDebuggerPresent, NtQueryInformationProcess, and timing checks. ScyllaHide patches these so the malware doesn't know it's being analyzed.

guides.title: How to Install and Use JADX
guides.description: This guide covers installing JADX and using it to decompile Android APK files back to readable Java source code.

steps.title: Install JADX
steps.description: Download JADX from GitHub releases or install via package manager.

commands.label: Download
commands.command:
# Download latest from https://github.com/skylot/jadx/releases
# Extract and run bin/jadx-gui

steps.title: Open an APK in the GUI
steps.description: Launch the GUI and load an APK file for visual exploration.

commands.label: Then
commands.command:
# File > Open > Select your .apk file
# Wait for decompilation
# Browse the source tree on the left

steps.notes: JADX decompiles DEX bytecode back to Java source. The output isn't perfect but it's usually very readable. You can also open .dex, .jar, and .class files.

steps.title: Command-Line Decompilation
steps.description: Decompile an APK to a directory of Java source files from the command line.
steps.notes: The --show-bad-code flag includes methods that couldn't be fully decompiled, showing the bytecode as comments. This is useful when analyzing obfuscated apps.

steps.title: Search and Navigate
steps.description: Find interesting code in the decompiled output.

commands.label: GUI search
commands.command:
# Navigation > Text Search (Ctrl+Shift+F)
# Search for: 'http://', 'api_key', 'password', 'secret'
# Navigation > Class Search (Ctrl+N) to find specific classes

steps.notes: Start by searching for URLs, API keys, hardcoded credentials, and encryption keys. Many Android apps contain secrets in their source that developers assumed wouldn't be accessible.

steps.title: Export for Further Analysis
steps.description: Export the decompiled source and resources for deeper analysis.
steps.notes: Exporting as a Gradle project lets you open the decompiled app in Android Studio for a full IDE experience with code navigation, search, and refactoring tools.

guides.title: How to Install and Use dnSpy
guides.description: This guide covers installing dnSpy on Windows and using it to decompile, debug, and modify .NET assemblies.
prerequisites: A .NET executable or DLL to analyze

steps.title: Install dnSpy
steps.description: Download dnSpy from GitHub releases. It's portable - no installer.

commands.label: Download
commands.command:
# Download latest from https://github.com/dnSpy/dnSpy/releases
# Extract zip
# Run dnSpy.exe

steps.notes: The original repo is archived but the tool still works perfectly. Several community forks continue development.

steps.title: Load and Decompile
steps.description: Open a .NET assembly and browse the decompiled C# source code.

commands.label: GUI steps
commands.command:
# File > Open > Select .exe or .dll
# Assembly tree appears on the left
# Click any class/method to see decompiled C# code
# Right-click > Go to Entry Point to find main()

steps.notes: dnSpy produces very clean C# output from compiled .NET assemblies. Variable names, method signatures, and string literals are all preserved because .NET metadata is rich.

steps.title: Debug Without Source
steps.description: Set breakpoints and debug .NET applications without having the source code. This is dnSpy's killer feature.

commands.label: Debug steps
commands.command:
# Debug > Start Debugging (F5) to launch the exe under debugger
# Or Debug > Attach to Process for running applications
# Click in the margin to set breakpoints
# F10 = Step Over, F11 = Step Into
# Locals/Watch windows show variable values

steps.title: Edit and Patch
steps.description: Modify IL code or C# directly and save the patched assembly.

commands.label: Edit steps
commands.command:
# Right-click a method > Edit Method Body (IL)
# Or Right-click a method > Edit Method (C#)
# Make changes in the editor
# File > Save Module to write the patched assembly

steps.notes: You can change string comparisons, bypass license checks, modify return values, or add logging. Save creates a new patched binary.

steps.title: Search Across Assemblies
steps.description: Search for strings, types, methods, and references across loaded assemblies.

commands.label: Search features
commands.command:
# Edit > Search Assemblies (Ctrl+Shift+K)
# Search for: passwords, API keys, crypto operations
# Analyze tab shows cross-references (who calls this method?)

steps.notes: For malware analysis, search for crypto-related classes (AES, RSA), network classes (HttpClient, Socket), and file operations (File.Write, Registry). These reveal the malware's capabilities quickly.

guides.title: How to Install and Use ILSpy
guides.description: This guide covers installing ILSpy and using it to decompile .NET assemblies to readable C# source code across all platforms.
prerequisites: .NET Runtime (for CLI tool) or Windows (for GUI)

steps.title: Install ILSpy
steps.description: ILSpy is available as a Windows GUI, VS Code extension, and cross-platform CLI tool.

steps.title: Decompile via CLI
steps.description: Decompile a .NET assembly to C# source files from the command line.

steps.title: GUI Navigation
steps.description: Open assemblies in the GUI and browse decompiled code.

commands.label: GUI steps
commands.command:
# File > Open > Select assembly
# Browse namespaces and types in the tree
# Click any type/method to see decompiled C#
# View > IL Code to see intermediate language alongside C#

steps.title: Search and Analyze
steps.description: Find specific code patterns across loaded assemblies.

commands.label: Search
commands.command:
# Edit > Search (F3)
# Search types, members, or constants
# Analyze window (Ctrl+R) shows 'Used By' and 'Uses' for any member

steps.notes: ILSpy focuses on decompilation quality. If you need debugging, use dnSpy instead. If you need cross-platform CLI decompilation, ILSpy is the better choice.

guides.title: How to Install and Use ngrok
guides.description: This guide covers installing ngrok and using it to expose local services to the internet for testing, webhooks, and demonstrations.
prerequisites: A free ngrok account (sign up at ngrok.com)

steps.title: Install and Authenticate
steps.description: Install ngrok and add your auth token.

commands.label: Linux
commands.command: curl -sSL https://ngrok-agent.s3.amazonaws.com/ngrok.asc | sudo tee /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/ngrok.asc >/dev/null && echo 'deb https://ngrok-agent.s3.amazonaws.com buster main' | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ngrok.list && sudo apt update && sudo apt install ngrok

steps.title: Expose a Local Web Server
steps.description: Create a public URL that tunnels to a local port.
steps.output:
Session Status: online
Forwarding: https://abc123.ngrok-free.app -> http://localhost:3000
steps.notes: The forwarding URL is publicly accessible. Anyone with the URL can reach your local server. The URL changes each time unless you have a paid plan with reserved domains.

steps.title: Expose Other Protocols
steps.description: ngrok supports TCP tunnels for SSH, databases, and other non-HTTP services.

steps.title: Inspect Traffic
steps.description: ngrok's built-in inspector shows all requests passing through the tunnel.

commands.label: Web inspector
commands.command:
# Visit http://localhost:4040 while ngrok is running
# See all requests, headers, bodies, and responses
# Replay requests to test changes

steps.notes: The traffic inspector at localhost:4040 is incredibly useful for debugging webhooks. You can see exactly what the remote service is sending and replay requests without triggering the webhook again.

steps.title: Security Considerations
steps.description: ngrok is a dual-use tool. Understand the risks.
steps.notes: ngrok tunnels bypass firewalls and NAT by design. In a corporate environment, outbound ngrok connections may violate security policy. Defenders should monitor for ngrok domains in DNS logs.

guides.title: How to Install and Use APKTool
guides.description: This guide covers installing APKTool and using it to decompile, modify, and rebuild Android APK files.

steps.title: Install APKTool
steps.description: Install APKTool from your package manager or download the jar.

steps.title: Decompile an APK
steps.description: Decode an APK into its components - smali code, resources, manifest, and assets.

commands.label: What you get
commands.command:
ls output/
# AndroidManifest.xml  - App permissions and components
# res/                 - Decoded resources (layouts, strings, images)
# smali/               - Dalvik bytecode in smali format
# assets/              - Raw asset files

steps.notes: APKTool decodes to smali (Dalvik assembly), not Java. Use JADX if you want Java source. APKTool is better when you need to modify and rebuild the APK.

steps.title: Analyze the Manifest
steps.description: The AndroidManifest.xml reveals permissions, activities, services, receivers, and exported components.
steps.notes: Look for exported components (exported=true) - these are accessible to other apps and are common attack surfaces. Excessive permissions are also a red flag.

steps.title: Modify and Rebuild
steps.description: Make changes to the decompiled APK and rebuild it.

commands.label: Sign the APK
commands.command:
keytool -genkey -v -keystore test.keystore -alias test -keyalg RSA -keysize 2048 -validity 10000
jarsigner -verbose -sigalg SHA1withRSA -digestalg SHA1 -keystore test.keystore modified.apk test

steps.notes: Rebuilt APKs must be signed to install on a device. The signature won't match the original, so apps with signature verification will detect the modification.

guides.title: How to Install and Use Radare2
guides.description: This guide covers installing Radare2 and using it for binary analysis, disassembly, and reverse engineering from the command line.

steps.title: Install Radare2
steps.description: Install r2 from package manager or build from source for the latest version.

steps.title: Open and Analyze a Binary
steps.description: Open a binary in r2 and run analysis to identify functions, strings, and cross-references.
steps.notes: The 'aaa' command runs all analysis passes. It takes a moment but gives you the most complete picture. 'afl' lists all identified functions with their sizes.

steps.title: Navigate and Disassemble
steps.description: Move through the binary and view disassembly.
steps.notes: VV enters visual graph mode showing control flow. Press 'p' to cycle through views, 'q' to quit visual mode. Visual mode is much easier to navigate than raw commands.

steps.title: Search for Patterns
steps.description: Search for strings, bytes, and code patterns in the binary.

steps.title: Useful Commands Cheatsheet
steps.description: Essential r2 commands for everyday use.

commands.label: Quick reference
commands.command:
# aaa - analyze all
# afl - list functions
# pdf - print disassembly of function
# iz - list strings
# VV - visual graph mode
# s addr - seek to address
# / string - search for string
# axt addr - cross-references to address
# px 64 - print 64 bytes hex dump
# ? - help for any command prefix

steps.notes: Radare2 has a steep learning curve but is incredibly powerful once you learn the commands. Every command can be followed by '?' for help (e.g., 'a?' shows all analysis commands).

guides.title: How to Install and Use osquery
guides.description: This guide covers installing osquery and using SQL queries to inspect your system's processes, network connections, users, and security state.

steps.title: Install osquery
steps.description: Install osquery from the official packages.

steps.title: Your First Queries
steps.description: Query your system like a database. Each table represents a different aspect of the OS.

commands.label: Running processes
commands.command: SELECT name, pid, path, cmdline FROM processes ORDER BY start_time DESC LIMIT 20;

commands.label: Listening ports
commands.command: SELECT p.name, l.port, l.address, l.protocol FROM listening_ports l JOIN processes p ON l.pid = p.pid;

commands.label: Logged-in users
commands.command: SELECT user, host, time FROM logged_in_users;

steps.title: Security Queries
steps.description: Queries specifically useful for security monitoring and incident response.

commands.label: Find processes running from /tmp
commands.command: SELECT name, path, pid FROM processes WHERE path LIKE '/tmp/%' OR path LIKE '/var/tmp/%';

commands.label: Check for crypto miners
commands.command: SELECT name, path, cmdline FROM processes WHERE cmdline LIKE '%stratum%' OR cmdline LIKE '%minerd%' OR name LIKE '%xmrig%';

commands.label: Recently modified files in sensitive dirs
commands.command: SELECT path, mtime, size FROM file WHERE directory IN ('/etc/cron.d', '/etc/cron.daily') AND mtime > (strftime('%s','now') - 86400);

steps.title: Network Investigation
steps.description: Query network connections, DNS, and ARP tables.

commands.label: Active connections
commands.command: SELECT p.name, p.pid, s.remote_address, s.remote_port, s.state FROM process_open_sockets s JOIN processes p ON s.pid = p.pid WHERE s.remote_port != 0 AND s.state = 'ESTABLISHED';

steps.notes: The process_open_sockets + processes join is one of the most useful queries for incident response - it shows you exactly which process is talking to which remote address.

steps.title: Schedule Queries (Daemon Mode)
steps.description: Run osquery as a daemon that executes queries on a schedule and logs the results.
steps.notes: In daemon mode, osquery runs scheduled queries and logs differential results (only new/changed rows). This is how it's used for continuous endpoint monitoring at scale.

guides.title: How to Install and Use Social Analyzer
guides.description: This guide covers installing Social Analyzer and using it to find and analyze social media profiles across hundreds of platforms.

steps.title: Install Social Analyzer
steps.description: Install from pip.

steps.title: Search for a Username
steps.description: Search for a username across all supported platforms.

commands.label: With specific platforms
commands.command: social-analyzer -t username --method find --websites github twitter instagram linkedin

steps.title: API Mode
steps.description: Run Social Analyzer as a web API for integration with other tools.
steps.notes: API mode is useful for integrating Social Analyzer into automated OSINT pipelines or custom investigation dashboards.

steps.title: Output and Reporting
steps.description: Save results in different formats.

guides.title: How to Install and Use Mimikatz
guides.description: This guide covers downloading Mimikatz and using it to extract credentials from Windows memory during authorized penetration tests.
prerequisites: Authorization to test the target system

steps.title: Obtain Mimikatz
steps.description: Download Mimikatz from the official GitHub repository. Note that antivirus will flag it.

commands.label: Download
commands.command:
# Download from https://github.com/gentilkiwi/mimikatz/releases
# Extract zip
# Run mimikatz.exe from an admin command prompt

commands.label: Or build from source
commands.command:
# Clone https://github.com/gentilkiwi/mimikatz
# Open mimikatz.sln in Visual Studio
# Build Release x64

steps.notes: Every AV product detects mimikatz. In real engagements, operators often compile from source with modifications, use Invoke-Mimikatz (PowerShell), or dump LSASS memory and analyze it offline. For lab practice, add an AV exclusion.

steps.title: Elevate Privileges
steps.description: Mimikatz needs debug privileges to access LSASS process memory.
steps.notes: This requires running as Administrator. If you get 'ERROR kuhl_m_privilege_simple', you don't have sufficient privileges.

steps.title: Dump Credentials
steps.description: Extract plaintext passwords, NTLM hashes, and Kerberos tickets from memory.
steps.notes: logonpasswords shows credentials for all logged-in users. On older Windows versions (pre-2012), you'll often get plaintext passwords. On newer versions, you get NTLM hashes which can be cracked or used in pass-the-hash attacks.

steps.title: Kerberos Attacks
steps.description: Extract and manipulate Kerberos tickets for lateral movement.
steps.notes: Golden tickets give you unrestricted access to every service in the domain. This requires the krbtgt account hash, which you can get after compromising a domain controller with lsadump::dcsync.

steps.title: DCSync Attack
steps.description: Replicate credentials from a domain controller remotely, without touching the DC's disk.
steps.notes: DCSync requires Domain Admin or Replication rights. It simulates a domain controller requesting password replication, pulling NTLM hashes without needing to log into the DC. This is the cleanest way to extract domain credentials.
